title: Topics on Foundations of Physics: From the quantum to the (semi) classical, gravity, thermodynamics, and (or beyond) our possible detections
abstract: The work leading to this thesis focuses on assessing and extending quantum theories in order to explore and test their implications across various regimes -- including thermodynamics, semiclassical and quantum gravity scenarios, and the in principle detectable predictions of such theories. The general motivation stems from a basic desire to understand the world form its very foundations. For instance, how can we bridge the gap between what we observe or `perceive' and the fundamental quantum nature in our theories. In particular, this work originated from the search to a better understanding of the nature of time according to our physical theories and the common perception that it invariably `flows' to the future, or, in other words, why do we observe distinct natural processes evolving asymmetrically in time? These motivations led to three distinct, yet interconnected and successful, lines of research, presented here in three separate parts: I. On Possible Detections within Physical Theories; II. On Explaining the Approach to Thermodynamic Equilibrium; and III. Relativistic Collapse Theories and a Self-Consistent Model of Semiclassical Gravity.
summary: This thesis tackles the foundations of physics by bridging quantum theory, thermodynamics, and gravity. It develops a novel, self-consistent semiclassical gravity model where quantum collapse dynamics dictate the source of spacetime curvature. The work aims to resolve fundamental issues like the arrow of time and the measurement problem through testable predictions for gravitational experiments.
main_problem: The central goal is to reconcile quantum mechanics with general relativity and to provide a fundamental understanding of physical laws, particularly addressing the arrow of time and the nature of measurement.
main_result: The work proposes a self-consistent semiclassical gravity framework where objective quantum collapse dynamics source the classical Einstein tensor, yielding testable predictions for gravitational entanglement experiments.
method: The approach involves analyzing foundational paradoxes (like absolute velocity detection) and developing a novel coupling mechanism between quantum collapse models and classical general relativity.
model_or_system: The models span Newtonian mechanics, de Broglie-Bohm pilot-wave theory, objective collapse theories (like GRW), and a semiclassical gravity framework coupling the expectation value of the energy-momentum tensor to the Einstein tensor.
key_observables: Relative phases in gravitationally-induced entanglement experiments, and the detectability of absolute velocities in hypothetical universes.
important_parameters: The energy-momentum density $	ext{Tab}(x)$ defining the source for gravity, and the time scale required for phase shifts ($	au \sim 3.5$s).
main_assumptions: The framework assumes that the expectation value of the energy-momentum tensor provides a Lorentz-invariant and self-consistent source for classical gravity, and that fundamental physical properties are, in principle, detectable.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the spacetime metric components ($ds^2$) for static, spherically symmetric systems, detailing the matching conditions across Core, Shell, and Exterior regions.
paper_structure: The thesis is structured in three interconnected parts: I) Investigating detectability in various regimes (Newtonian/QM), II) Explaining the approach to thermodynamic equilibrium, and III) Developing the self-consistent semiclassical gravity model.
why_interesting: While highly theoretical, the focus on objective collapse models and their coupling to gravity provides a unique, non-standard extension to quantum dynamics that could inform open quantum systems research by providing a mechanism for decoherence/collapse in a relativistic setting.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 6.1: Spacetime diagram of the EPR setting with two foliations: in yellow th one with hypersurfaces Σt and in blue the one with hypersurfaces Σt′. A and B represent the events associated with Alice and Bob’s measurements in the z−spin basis respectively. P is an event in Alice’s worldline, before she makes her measurement, and R is an event in Bob’s worldline, after he makes his measurement.

Figure 8.1: Different foliations sharing the same hypersurface Σtf. Independently of which foliation is used to arrive to the state in Σtf, e.g. the one that contains Σt′ with the associated state ρ′, or Σt′′ with ρ′′, the theory should assign the same ρ(Σtf) to Σtf. And any differences between the states ρ′ and ρ0 are due to what happens in Γ.
Figure 8.2: Matter density of two pointers in the superposition (8.17) showing just the x spatial dimension of Σ0.

Figure 8.3: Spacetime diagram showing the hypersurfaces Σ0, Σt, Σt′ and Σt′′. The mass density is shown in red with transparency for m/2 and full color for m after detection, e.g. to the right, R1 and R2.

Figure 9.1: An spherically symmetric superposition of masses suffers a collapse at r = 0 and t = 0, localizing the entire mass M at the region of the shell. The central massive core has radius RC and uniform density ρC, and the shell has an inner radius RI, an outer radius RE and uniform density ρS.
